subject: ap-calculus-bc
theme: Derivatives become practical tools when applied to motion, rates of change in linked quantities, linear approximation, and evaluating indeterminate limits.

core_concepts.title: Interpreting Derivatives in Context
core_concepts.detail: The derivative $f'(t)$ gives the instantaneous rate of change of $f$ at time $t$. Always include units: if $f$ is in gallons and $t$ is in minutes, then $f'(t)$ is in gallons per minute.

core_concepts.title: Position, Velocity, Acceleration
core_concepts.detail: For motion along a line: $v(t) = s'(t)$ (velocity), $a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t)$ (acceleration), speed $= |v(t)|$. A particle slows down when $v$ and $a$ have opposite signs.

core_concepts.title: Related Rates
core_concepts.detail: When two or more quantities change over time and are related by an equation, differentiate both sides with respect to $t$ to relate their rates. Always identify knowns and unknowns before substituting.

core_concepts.title: Linearization
core_concepts.detail: The tangent line $L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$ gives a linear approximation of $f$ near $x = a$. The approximation is better when $x$ is closer to $a$.

core_concepts.title: L'Hôpital's Rule
core_concepts.detail: If $\lim_{x \to c} \frac{f(x)}{g(x)}$ is in the form $\frac{0}{0}$ or $\frac{\infty}{\infty}$, then the limit equals $\lim_{x \to c} \frac{f'(x)}{g'(x)}$ (provided this limit exists). Verify the indeterminate form first.

formulas.name: Velocity and Acceleration
formulas.description: Velocity is the derivative of position, acceleration is the derivative of velocity. Speed is the absolute value of velocity — always non-negative.
formulas.example: If $s(t) = t^3 - 6t$, then $v(t) = 3t^2 - 6$ and $a(t) = 6t$. At $t = 1$: $v(1) = -3$ (moving left), speed $= 3$.

formulas.name: Linearization
formulas.description: The tangent line at x = a approximates f(x) for values near a. This is the same as the first-degree Taylor polynomial.

formulas.name: L'Hôpital's Rule
formulas.description: Replaces the original limit with the ratio of derivatives. Can be applied repeatedly if the result is still indeterminate. Always verify the form before applying.

exam_tip: In related rates problems, draw a diagram, write an equation relating the quantities, then differentiate both sides with respect to $t$ before substituting values. Speed is $|v(t)|$, not $v(t)$ — a particle slows down when velocity and acceleration have opposite signs.
